skill.name: py-async-patterns
skill.description: Async and await guidance for Python services using FastAPI and SQLAlchemy, including sessions, transactions, concurrency, and async debugging patterns.
skill.summary: Apply reliable async patterns for FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, database sessions, transactions, and concurrent operations.
skill.author: CJHarmath

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the flagged backticks are markdown fences or inline examples, not executable Ruby or shell code. The only real command examples are local grep and ruff checks for async code review, and no prompt injection, data exfiltration, destructive command, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: FALSE_POSITIVE: Markdown code fences interpreted as shell execution
low_findings.description: The static Ruby or shell backtick findings point to markdown code fences and inline code examples. These lines document Python async patterns and do not execute commands or evaluate user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are markdown delimiters, Python snippets, or table inline code. No executable Ruby backtick expression or shell invocation appears at these locations.

low_findings.title: FALSE_POSITIVE: Database URL placeholder in documentation
low_findings.description: The DATABASE_URL reference is an illustrative SQLAlchemy engine parameter in a documentation code block. No code reads environment variables, prints secrets, or sends credentials elsewhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The value appears inside a fenced Python example for create_async_engine. There is no process environment access or network exfiltration behavior in the skill file.

low_findings.title: FALSE_POSITIVE: Weak crypto and reconnaissance keyword matches
low_findings.description: The high and low blocker matches are normal text or SQLAlchemy examples about async code, database queries, sorting IDs, and post-condition checks. No evidence found for weak cryptographic algorithms, host reconnaissance, or system inventory collection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is instructional Python documentation. The flagged lines do not contain cryptographic APIs, operating system probes, shell discovery commands, or sensitive data collection.

low_findings.title: LOW_RISK: Local detection commands are documented for user repositories
low_findings.description: The skill includes grep and ruff commands to find async session leaks, sequential queries, and dangling tasks. These are non-destructive local inspection commands, but they may be run in a user repository and should be treated as command examples.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and limited to grep and ruff checks. They do not modify files, access the network, or collect secrets, but they are still external command examples.

content.value_statement: Async Python services can develop session leaks, stale data, slow endpoints, and transaction bugs. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical FastAPI and SQLAlchemy patterns for safer async work.
actual_capabilities: Explains request-scoped AsyncSession lifecycle patterns for FastAPI dependencies.
Compares concurrent and sequential SQLAlchemy query patterns using asyncio.gather.
Shows transaction boundaries with commit, rollback, begin, and refresh guidance.
Describes connection pool tradeoffs and session closing practices for async engines.
Covers background task choices, deadlock prevention, validation, and structured logging.
Provides local grep and ruff checks for common async code issues.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not include executable scripts or automated refactoring tools.
Examples require adaptation to each project schema, session factory, and dependency setup.
Guidance focuses on FastAPI, SQLAlchemy, and asyncio rather than every Python async framework.
It does not replace load testing, database profiling, or production incident review.

use_cases.title: Review FastAPI database sessions
use_cases.description: Check whether request handlers scope AsyncSession objects correctly and close sessions after each request.

use_cases.title: Improve async endpoint latency
use_cases.description: Identify independent database reads that can run concurrently without breaking dependency order.

use_cases.title: Debug async data consistency issues
use_cases.description: Use transaction, refresh, lock ordering, and validation patterns to reason about stale data or deadlocks.

prompt_templates.title: Check session lifecycle
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one FastAPI endpoint.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this FastAPI endpoint for AsyncSession lifecycle issues. Explain whether the session is request scoped and where leaks could occur.

prompt_templates.title: Find safe concurrency
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these async SQLAlchemy queries. Separate independent queries from dependent queries and suggest where concurrency is safe.

prompt_templates.title: Audit transaction handling
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of a multi-step write operation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this async write workflow for commit, rollback, refresh, and transaction boundary problems. Recommend a safer structure.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose production async failures
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced debugging of latency, stale data, and deadlocks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the py-async-patterns guidance to diagnose these symptoms and logs. Prioritize likely async session, pool, concurrency, or lock ordering causes.

output_examples.input: A FastAPI route creates a shared AsyncSession and reuses it across requests.
output_examples.output: The review flags a session lifecycle issue, explains stale data and connection leak risks, and recommends a request-scoped dependency.

output_examples.input: A dashboard endpoint awaits three independent database queries one after another.
output_examples.output: The review separates independent work from dependent work and recommends concurrent execution only where the queries do not share results.

output_examples.input: A transfer workflow updates two records and commits after each update.
output_examples.output: The review recommends one transaction boundary, rollback on failure, consistent lock ordering, and post-commit validation.

best_practices: Keep AsyncSession instances scoped to one request or unit of work.
Run independent async operations concurrently only when they do not depend on shared results.
Validate important post-conditions after commits, refreshes, and background operations.
anti_patterns: Do not store a global AsyncSession or reuse one session across requests.
Do not parallelize queries that depend on values loaded by earlier queries.
Do not create fire-and-forget tasks for work that must finish before the response.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill with Python examples and a few local inspection command examples.

faq.question: Which frameworks does it focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on FastAPI, SQLAlchemy AsyncSession, asyncio, and common async service patterns.

faq.question: Can it fix async bugs automatically?
faq.answer: No. It helps an AI assistant review code and recommend changes, but edits still depend on the project context.

faq.question: Is asyncio.gather always recommended?
faq.answer: No. The skill recommends concurrency only for independent operations that do not require earlier query results.

faq.question: Does it cover database transactions?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes commit, rollback, context manager, refresh, and multi-step operation guidance.

faq.question: Why are external commands listed as a risk factor?
faq.answer: The skill documents grep and ruff commands for local code inspection. They are non-destructive but still external command examples.
